text: The surgeon begins the procedure with a 5mm port in the epigastric region, observing dilated proximal bowel loops and collapsed distal ileal loops. Adhesions of the small bowel to the anterior abdominal wall are visible, likely near a previous operative scar. The surgeon carefully performs adhesiolysis using scissors and atraumatic graspers, gently pulling the bowel downward to reveal the dissection plane. The peritoneum is dissected along with the bowel to prevent injury, with minor bleeding observed that is expected to self-resolve.

The surgeon completes adhesiolysis on one loop of the bowel, revealing another loop adhered to a previous operative scar. The adjacent peritoneum is carefully dissected away from the bowel surface to avoid injury. After checking for hemostasis and any inadvertent tears, the surgeon identifies a thick fibrous band holding the anti-mesenteric border of the terminal ileal loop. The band, minimally vascular, extends underneath and connects to another loop of bowel. The surgeon cautiously divides the band using a harmonic shear.

After dividing the fibrous band, the surgeon begins tracing the bowel from the ileocecal junction, observing the collapsed loops of bowel. Using two atraumatic bowel graspers, the surgeon gently pulls the bowel, noting healthy bowel loops and visible peristalsis. The previously obscured band is now visible, connecting the distal antimesentery border of the jejunum with the antimesentery border of the terminal ileum, which was causing the obstruction.

The surgeon traces the proximal dilated bowel loops, ensuring that only adhesions causing or potentially causing obstruction are addressed. The video shows the port sites from previous surgeries, including appendectomy and segmental colectomy.
